definition: Distraction through aversion, hostility, or ill will toward what is resisted.
notes: Project preference is aversive distraction within the nīvaraṇa framework so the relation to distraction remains explicit. Ill will remains a clear alternate when explanatory prose wants the more familiar ethical label. The term should be heard as aversion functioning specifically as a disturbance of practice and composure. The OSF glossary confirms aversive distraction as the house default inside the five-distractions framework and preserves ill will as the explanatory alternate.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose emphasizing hostility toward others or situations
context_rules.notes: Acceptable alternate when the aversive tone needs a more familiar phrase.

context_rules.context: When the passage emphasizes aversion as a distraction to be abandoned in meditation
context_rules.notes: Keep the family relation to the other nīvaraṇa terms explicit.

example_phrases.notes: Standard member of the fivefold list.

example_phrases.translation: a mind free from aversive distraction

authority_basis.scope: Supports keeping aversive distraction as the house default inside the five-hindrances framework, with ill will retained as the explanatory alternate.

translation_policy.default_scope: Use 'aversive distraction' as the default rendering in the entry's governed translation contexts unless a recorded context rule overrides it.
translation_policy.when_not_to_apply: Yield to recorded context_rules, source-facing technical use, or other explicitly documented local reasons rather than rotating among alternates ad hoc.
translation_policy.drift_risk: Prevents drift back toward 'anger' or other unrecorded alternates.
